ending: Broken Homes ends on the series' hardest gut-punch. The Skygarden case resolves: Erik Stromberg built the tower as a magical machine, quietly accumulating power over decades, and the Faceless Man engineered the estate's decant and demolition in order to harvest that stored charge for himself. On demolition night the tower comes down, and in the chaos the truth comes out - Lesley May, the Folly's second apprentice and Peter's closest friend, has been working for the Faceless Man. She tasers Peter and escapes with the enemy, her betrayal apparently bought with the one thing the Folly could not give her: the promise of repairing her destroyed face. Varvara Sidorovna, the Soviet-trained battle magician who fought on the Faceless Man's side of the affair, ends the book in the Folly's hands after facing Nightingale - proof of how far outclassed almost everyone is when the old soldiers fight. The Faceless Man himself remains at large, better resourced and more dangerous than the Folly had credited, and now with an apprentice-grade insider's knowledge of their people and methods. On the personal side, Beverley Brook is back in London, she and Peter finally acting on the pull between them. Peter ends the book physically intact and personally wrecked: the hunt for the Faceless Man is now also the hunt for Lesley, and the Folly must reckon with how long she was turned and how much she gave away.
in_short: A fatal car crash with a corpse in the vehicle, a town-planning official who steps in front of a train, and a rare-book thief burned from the inside all prove to share a thread: the Skygarden Estate, a brutalist south London tower designed by Erik Stromberg, an architect who knew about magic. Peter Grant and Lesley May move onto the estate undercover and discover that Stromberg built the tower as a slow magical accumulator - and that the Faceless Man, the trained black magician the Folly has hunted for two books, intends to harvest its stored power by having the tower demolished. Along the way Peter meets Sky, a nymph bound to the estate's trees, crosses Varvara Sidorovna, a Soviet-trained Night Witch working for the enemy, and welcomes Beverley Brook back from her upstream exile, their relationship finally beginning. On demolition night the tower falls and the case detonates personally: Lesley May has been the Faceless Man's agent, her loyalty bought with the promise of a repaired face. She tasers Peter and vanishes with him. Varvara is captured after facing Nightingale; the Faceless Man escapes; and the Folly is left betrayed from within.

recaps.text: Previously: Peter Grant, a young Met constable, became apprentice to Thomas Nightingale, the last sanctioned wizard in England, after the ghost-driven Covent Garden killings - a case that ended with Nightingale shot and Peter's friend Lesley May possessed, the spirit's departure leaving her face in ruins. Peter brokered a truce between the rival deities of the Thames, sealed by an exchange of hostages that sent Beverley Brook, the river spirit he had grown close to, upstream to Father Thames's court. The jazz vampire case then revealed a live threat: the Faceless Man, a trained, ruthless black magician schooled through Geoffrey Wheatcroft's illegal Oxford teaching and the Little Crocodiles dining club - he fought Peter and escaped, and hunting him became the Folly's long game. It also cost Peter the woman he loved: Simone and her sisters, revenants of the 1941 Cafe de Paris bombing who had unknowingly fed on jazz musicians for decades, were laid to rest. Since then the Folly has grown: Lesley, mask and all, was accepted as Nightingale's second apprentice and now trains beside Peter, and a murder beneath the Underground drew the Folly into London's wider demi-monde, including the Quiet People, a hidden community living under the city. The Faceless Man remains at large.

The book opens as a scatter of cases that refuse to look connected. A car crash on a rain-soaked road turns out to have a corpse in the vehicle that did not die in the accident, and the driver's associations are strange enough to interest the Folly. A mild, apparently contented town-planning official steps in front of a train for no reason anyone can find. A rare-book thief dies horribly - burned from the inside - after handling stolen goods that include a German grimoire, the working notebook of someone trained in the Newtonian tradition. Peter and Lesley, now working as a practiced team under Nightingale, chase the threads through the ordinary machinery of London policing: inquests, case files, planning records, stolen-property markets. The only common note is a faint, persistent suggestion of the Faceless Man's involvement - his organization, his interests, his willingness to spend lives on housekeeping - without any visible motive connecting a murdered stranger, a dead planner, and an old book. Folly life continues around the casework: training, Molly's increasingly adventurous cooking, and the constant background labour of rebuilding an institution that spent half a century running down.

The threads knot around a place. The stolen grimoire proves to have belonged to Erik Stromberg, a German-born modernist architect who emigrated to Britain and whose papers show he knew about magic - and the dead planner's decisions connect to the same file: Stromberg's most ambitious surviving work, the Skygarden Estate in south London, a brutalist tower long slated for demolition. Every strand the Folly pulls - the crash victim's associations, the book's provenance, the planning history easing the estate toward the wrecking ball - runs through Skygarden or the property interests circling it. The working theory hardens: Stromberg built something into that tower, the Faceless Man knows it, and the demolition is not urban renewal but somebody's endgame. Since the Folly cannot watch a whole estate from outside, Peter and Lesley go in: they move into the tower undercover as new residents, posing as ordinary Londoners, to learn what the building actually is and who else is paying attention to it. Living inside the case, they start mapping the estate's odd corners - and its odd residents - from the inside.

Undercover life on the estate settles into a rhythm of surveillance and small discoveries. The tower reveals itself slowly: design choices that make no sense as housing - the layout, the materials, the way the structure concentrates and channels its own strange atmosphere - start to look deliberate, the work of an architect designing for magic rather than people. Among the residents Peter gets to know Sky, a guileless young nymph bound up with the estate's trees and green spaces, living evidence that Skygarden is more ecosystem than failed building. The season also brings the river world back into Peter's life: at the spring gathering where the two courts of the Thames meet in their slowly warming truce, Beverley Brook returns from her upstream exile, and she and Peter pick up what the hostage exchange interrupted - this time with intent. Around the edges, the property-management interests running the estate's decant look less like bureaucracy and more like cover, and the demolition date keeps creeping closer while the Folly still cannot say what, exactly, the Faceless Man expects to gain when the tower comes down.

The picture completes, and it is worse than expected. Stromberg's tower is a machine: an accumulator, patiently gathering and storing magical potential over the decades of lives lived inside it. The demolition is the harvest - collapse the structure at the right moment, in the right way, and everything the building has banked can be captured by whoever is prepared for it. The Faceless Man is prepared: the emptying of the estate, the planning decisions, the deaths that opened the book were all groundwork. The opposition also acquires a face - Varvara Sidorovna, a Soviet-trained battle magician of the wartime Night Witches, decades old and lethally capable, is working the same ground for the other side, and encounters with her make plain the Folly is up against professionals. As the final residents are cleared and the charges are set, Peter and Lesley's undercover posting turns into a countdown: the Folly must decide whether to stop the demolition, spring the trap, or fight for control of the harvest itself, knowing the Faceless Man has out-planned them at every prior step and that whatever happens on demolition night will happen fast.

Demolition night brings the tower down and the case apart. The Faceless Man's harvest goes ahead: the Skygarden tower is destroyed, and in the confusion of evacuation, sabotage, and open magical combat, the Folly fails to take him. Varvara Sidorovna ends up facing Nightingale himself - a mismatch even for a Night Witch - and finishes the affair in the Folly's custody. But the night's real detonation is personal. Lesley May, the Folly's second apprentice, Peter's partner and closest friend, reveals that she has been working for the Faceless Man: she tasers Peter and leaves with the enemy. The plainest reading of her price is the one thing the Folly could never promise her - the restoration of the face the possession destroyed. The betrayal reframes everything behind it: how long she was turned, what she passed on, which of the Folly's setbacks were engineered with inside knowledge. The Faceless Man escapes the night richer, better informed, and with a trained apprentice at his side; the Folly ends it with a prisoner, a demolished crime scene, and a wound that will drive the series forward. Peter is left with Beverley, a recovering institution, and the knowledge that the hunt for the Faceless Man is now also the hunt for Lesley.
